text: You see a name twice in the results. That's how rivalries start.
It wasn't supposed to be personal, but it is now.
You spot a familiar face in the field. The field feels sharper already.
The rivalry doesn't need a headline. It's in the warmup.
They are in the same heat again, and the air gets tighter.
You spot them during strides, and your focus sharpens.
No words needed; the matchup explains itself.
Your rival got this one. You'll remember it.
They beat you today. That won't be the last meeting.
You finish ahead of your rival. It counts more than the result sheet says.
You got them today. The look they give you says they'll be back.
Same name keeps showing up next to yours. You notice.
You see them in warmups. That's the first time it feels real.
You remember their face from last week. Now you remember their time too.
They got you today. You file it away.
You shake hands, then walk off thinking about the rematch.
It stings, but it's clean. That's the part that stays with you.
Teammates talk about it on the bus. You just nod.
Beat your rival today. The season feels lighter.
You got them. That's momentum you can feel.
Week one. New season, same nerves.
First meet of the year. The body feels raw but ready.
You can still smell the new spikes in the bag.
Mid-season. The training is starting to show up in the times.
Week four. You're finding your shape.
Final regular season meet. You want to finish sharp.
Last league date before things get real.
Week eight. The season's in your body now.
Last regular meet. You want to be clean, not flashy.
It's been a long stretch. You still line up.
Four hundred hurdles is a rhythm event disguised as a sprint.
Today it's not about bravery. It's about precision.
You count strides between hurdles until you stop counting and trust the rhythm.
The hurdles don't care how tired your legs are in the final straight. You still have to clear them.
One bad hurdle reshapes everything that follows. You know this going in.
The back straight is where the legs start arguing with the stride pattern. You try not to let it break.
the 400 hurdles asks for precision before it asks for courage.
You simplify the race plan and trust the work.
The first segment feels controlled; the hard part is staying that way.
You pick a rhythm and refuse to let the noise pull you out of it.
Small technical choices in the 400 hurdles become big outcomes late.
You stay patient while the race shape develops around you.
The middle phase of the 400 hurdles is where composure pays off.
You notice the field, then bring your focus back to your own pattern.
Execution in the 400 hurdles is rarely loud, but it is always visible.
Ten hurdles in thirteen seconds. The rhythm either holds or it doesn't.
You're not jumping over them - you're running through them as efficiently as possible.
The first three steps determine the shape of everything after. The hurdles just confirm it.
You clip one and feel the correction ripple through the next four. That's the cost.
the 110 hurdles asks for precision before it asks for courage.
You simplify the race plan and trust the work.
The first segment feels controlled; the hard part is staying that way.
You pick a rhythm and refuse to let the noise pull you out of it.
Small technical choices in the 110 hurdles become big outcomes late.
You stay patient while the race shape develops around you.
The middle phase of the 110 hurdles is where composure pays off.
You notice the field, then bring your focus back to your own pattern.
Execution in the 110 hurdles is rarely loud, but it is always visible.
You keep your shoulders calm and your effort honest.
There is a moment where the 110 hurdles asks for commitment, and it is here.
Ten seconds to be exactly what you are.
100 meters. No tactics. No negotiation. Just speed.
Stay tall through the drive phase and trust the next ten steps.
The start is violent, then the race turns into pure mechanics.
Every tiny mistake shows up at this speed, so nothing can drift.
You do not search for rhythm in the hundred; you impose it.
By halfway, the winner is usually the one who stayed clean.
You hit max velocity and try to hold it one breath longer.
No room for correction here, only execution.
The race feels over almost as soon as it begins.
Block clearance is clean, and now it is all posture and frequency.
The crowd blurs; the lane lines are all that matter.
Your arms set the tempo and your legs have to obey.
This race rewards commitment, not hesitation.
One sharp transition can separate first from fourth.
The 200 rewards commitment around the bend.
Drive hard into the turn. Relax. Let it open up.
You come off the curve and feel where your speed is - whether it's still there or already gone.
The stagger makes it hard to read the field. You run your own race and find out later how it placed.
There's a moment coming out of the bend where you think you can hold your form. You usually can't.
Longer than the hundred by a margin that matters. The last fifty meters ask a different question.
the 200 asks for precision before it asks for courage.
You simplify the race plan and trust the work.
The first segment feels controlled; the hard part is staying that way.
You pick a rhythm and refuse to let the noise pull you out of it.
Small technical choices in the 200 become big outcomes late.
You stay patient while the race shape develops around you.
The middle phase of the 200 is where composure pays off.
You notice the field, then bring your focus back to your own pattern.
Execution in the 200 is rarely loud, but it is always visible.
A 400 is a negotiation with the back stretch.
Four hundred meters of math you can't solve until you're in it.
You know what the back straight costs. You've paid it before.
The first two hundred are a promise you have to keep for the second two.
Somewhere around two-fifty you stop thinking and just run. That's when it gets honest.
It's too long to sprint. It's too short to manage. The four hundred doesn't resolve that contradiction.
the 400 asks for precision before it asks for courage.
You simplify the race plan and trust the work.
The first segment feels controlled; the hard part is staying that way.
You pick a rhythm and refuse to let the noise pull you out of it.
Small technical choices in the 400 become big outcomes late.
You stay patient while the race shape develops around you.
The middle phase of the 400 is where composure pays off.
You notice the field, then bring your focus back to your own pattern.
Execution in the 400 is rarely loud, but it is always visible.
Two laps. Fast enough to hurt, far enough to require a plan.
The 800 breaks the impatient. You've learned to wait.
You go out with the pack and try to remember that the race doesn't really start for another two hundred meters.
First lap is bookkeeping. The second lap is the accounting.
You know roughly where you need to be at the bell. You try to get there without spending too much.
The kick only works if you've saved something for it. You think you have.
the 800 asks for precision before it asks for courage.
You simplify the race plan and trust the work.
The first segment feels controlled; the hard part is staying that way.
You pick a rhythm and refuse to let the noise pull you out of it.
Small technical choices in the 800 become big outcomes late.
You stay patient while the race shape develops around you.
The middle phase of the 800 is where composure pays off.
You notice the field, then bring your focus back to your own pattern.
Execution in the 800 is rarely loud, but it is always visible.
Four laps. The race that punishes bad pacing twice.
The mile has history. You're about to add to it.
Four laps. You know which one will be the hard one. It's always the third.
The pace feels controlled until it doesn't. You try to notice the difference early.
There's a point in the third lap where the rational part of your brain steps back. The legs carry the rest.
You don't count laps. Your body counts them for you.
the 1600 asks for precision before it asks for courage.
You simplify the race plan and trust the work.
The first segment feels controlled; the hard part is staying that way.
You pick a rhythm and refuse to let the noise pull you out of it.
Small technical choices in the 1600 become big outcomes late.
You stay patient while the race shape develops around you.
The middle phase of the 1600 is where composure pays off.
You notice the field, then bring your focus back to your own pattern.
Execution in the 1600 is rarely loud, but it is always visible.
Eight laps. The race that doesn't start until you're already tired.
Distance running is about what's left when everything else is gone.
Eight laps is a long time to know exactly what you signed up for.
You settle in early because there's no other option. The race will come to you.
By the fifth lap, you've had time to question several decisions. You change none of them.
The last half-mile is a negotiation between what your body wants and what the clock requires.
the 3200 asks for precision before it asks for courage.
You simplify the race plan and trust the work.
The first segment feels controlled; the hard part is staying that way.
You pick a rhythm and refuse to let the noise pull you out of it.
Small technical choices in the 3200 become big outcomes late.
You stay patient while the race shape develops around you.
The middle phase of the 3200 is where composure pays off.
You notice the field, then bring your focus back to your own pattern.
Execution in the 3200 is rarely loud, but it is always visible.
The shot put rewards commitment. You can't fake the throw.
One explosive second. The ring is small. The margin is not.
You stand in the circle, run through the motion one more time in your head, then stop thinking.
The technique has to happen without being supervised. That's what all the practice was for.
You release and know immediately, before you look. The body reports before the eyes do.
It lands. The flag goes up or it doesn't. You have two more attempts.
shot put asks for precision before it asks for courage.
You simplify the race plan and trust the work.
The first segment feels controlled; the hard part is staying that way.
You pick a rhythm and refuse to let the noise pull you out of it.
Small technical choices in shot put become big outcomes late.
You stay patient while the race shape develops around you.
The middle phase of shot put is where composure pays off.
You notice the field, then bring your focus back to your own pattern.
Execution in shot put is rarely loud, but it is always visible.
The discus is a timing event disguised as a strength event.
Spin, release, watch it carry. The good ones look effortless.
The spin happens in a smaller space than people expect. So does the whole throw.
Release too early and it drifts left. Too late and it collapses short. The window is smaller than it feels.
It leaves your hand and for a half-second belongs to physics entirely.
You've thrown this disc in practice more times than you can count. Today is one more.
discus asks for precision before it asks for courage.
You simplify the race plan and trust the work.
The first segment feels controlled; the hard part is staying that way.
You pick a rhythm and refuse to let the noise pull you out of it.
Small technical choices in discus become big outcomes late.
You stay patient while the race shape develops around you.
The middle phase of discus is where composure pays off.
You notice the field, then bring your focus back to your own pattern.
Execution in discus is rarely loud, but it is always visible.
Javelin is speed and timing. The runway sets the throw.
Fast approach, clean release, long flight.
The run-up is part of the throw. You have to arrive at the right speed, not the fastest one.
You feel the javelin's weight shift just before release and try to work with it, not against it.
There's a clean throw and a thrown throw. You know which one this was before it lands.
You watch the arc and read it. That's all you can do from here.
javelin asks for precision before it asks for courage.
You simplify the race plan and trust the work.
The first segment feels controlled; the hard part is staying that way.
You pick a rhythm and refuse to let the noise pull you out of it.
Small technical choices in javelin become big outcomes late.
You stay patient while the race shape develops around you.
The middle phase of javelin is where composure pays off.
You notice the field, then bring your focus back to your own pattern.
Execution in javelin is rarely loud, but it is always visible.
The runway is yours. Aggressive to the board.
Long jump is a sprint with a payoff at the end.
The runway is short enough that you never quite settle before the board arrives.
You hit the board and your job becomes staying long in the air - which is mostly about what happened before you left the ground.
The pit is honest. It doesn't account for the approach or the wind. It just gives you a number.
You land and look back at the mark, as if it might tell you something you don't already know.
long jump asks for precision before it asks for courage.
You simplify the race plan and trust the work.
The first segment feels controlled; the hard part is staying that way.
You pick a rhythm and refuse to let the noise pull you out of it.
Small technical choices in long jump become big outcomes late.
You stay patient while the race shape develops around you.
The middle phase of long jump is where composure pays off.
You notice the field, then bring your focus back to your own pattern.
Execution in long jump is rarely loud, but it is always visible.
A bar at a height you've cleared in practice. Do it again.
High jump lives and dies by approach rhythm. Find it early.
You walk the approach, counting steps, then stop and let the bar just exist for a moment.
The crossbar is set. You either clear it or you don't. There's no partial credit.
Your approach tempo has to be right before any of the rest of it matters.
You arch over the bar and feel the air just below it. You don't know how close it was until you're already down.
high jump asks for precision before it asks for courage.
You simplify the race plan and trust the work.
The first segment feels controlled; the hard part is staying that way.
You pick a rhythm and refuse to let the noise pull you out of it.
Small technical choices in high jump become big outcomes late.
You stay patient while the race shape develops around you.
The middle phase of high jump is where composure pays off.
You notice the field, then bring your focus back to your own pattern.
Execution in high jump is rarely loud, but it is always visible.
Hop, step, jump. Three decisions made in under three seconds.
The triple jump punishes hesitation.
You've rehearsed the sequence so many times it should be automatic. On the runway it never quite feels that way.
The hop sets up the step. The step sets up the jump. A bad start compounds over all three.
You land in the pit with the sense that it should have been longer.
Three contacts with the ground and then it's done. You stand up and check the flag.
triple jump asks for precision before it asks for courage.
You simplify the race plan and trust the work.
The first segment feels controlled; the hard part is staying that way.
You pick a rhythm and refuse to let the noise pull you out of it.
Small technical choices in triple jump become big outcomes late.
You stay patient while the race shape develops around you.
The middle phase of triple jump is where composure pays off.
You notice the field, then bring your focus back to your own pattern.
Execution in triple jump is rarely loud, but it is always visible.
You settle into the blocks and the world goes quiet in a way that has nothing to do with sound.
The starter holds you there longer than feels fair. You wait. Your calves know what to do.
Ten seconds from now it will be over. You have run this race a thousand times. Not once has it felt the same.
You press your fingers into the track and feel the give of it, the grain, the heat absorbed since morning.
The gun goes and you don't hear it - you just find yourself moving, already several steps in.
Your lungs don't factor in. There isn't time for them to matter.
At sixty meters you know roughly how it went. The last forty are just the body finishing what it started.
You cross the line and slow yourself against your will, legs still searching for a finish that already happened.
Another league meet. These are the reps that build the season.
The stands are quiet. The work is not.
League meets bleed together by mid-season. You keep showing up anyway.
Nobody remembers league meets in June. Your legs will.
Small crowd, familiar lanes, honest work. That is the whole assignment.
The bus ride is short and the warmup is shorter. League track.
You check the heat sheet out of habit. Same schools, same names, same job.
There is no banner for winning a Tuesday. You race like there is.
League meets are where form gets built and bad habits get exposed. Today does both.
The timers, the clipboard coaches, the kid selling candy bars by the fence. League track has a rhythm of its own.
Overcast and windless. A workmanlike day for a workmanlike meet.
The PA crackles through the same announcements it makes every week. You stretch through all of them.
Someone's little brother is racing spikes-in-hand down the infield again. League meets.
Two schools' worth of tents, one long afternoon, and a schedule that always runs late.
The long jumpers rake the pit while the distance kids jog the fence line. Everything in its place.
You know this track's soft spots and where the wind sneaks in at the turn. Home-circuit knowledge.
An official waves down a false start in the youth heat. The afternoon settles into its usual shape.
Your warmup is muscle memory by now. That is not boredom -- that is craft.
Same drills, same order, same lane assignments taped to the fence. You find comfort in the routine.
The team jogs its lap, circles up, breaks it down. Week in, week out.
You eat the same pre-meet snack at the same time. Superstition or science, it is undefeated.
Coach gives the same speech with different names in it. You listen anyway.
Check in, get your hip number, find a patch of shade. The rituals hold the season together.
Somewhere between the second and third league meet, this stopped being new and started being yours.
Club championship day. Everyone thinks they peaked this week.
The heat sheets look different. So do the warm-ups.
Club champs. The first time it feels like it counts.
You can hear the timers click louder today.
It isn't State, but it isn't a normal meet either.
Class meet. The first real filter. You've been pointing here.
Every drill, every rep -- it narrows to this.
Class meet. Mistakes cost spots now.
You keep your warmup tight. You don't want surprises here.
It feels like the season just got real.
State meet morning. You breathe slower than usual.
The best in the state. You've earned the right to find out where you stand.
State meet. The infield looks bigger, the lanes feel tighter.
You hear your name called and your stomach drops. Then you breathe.
This is the meet people talk about at school. You're in it.
Regional. The competition is different now -- everyone here wanted this.
You look at the names in your heat. You recognize a few. So do they.
Regionals. The pace is honest from the gun.
There aren't soft heats anymore. Everyone here can perform.
You feel a different kind of focus in the warmup area.
National championships. The end of everything you've built.
You've put in thousands of reps to stand on this line. One more.
Nationals. The warmup is quiet and serious.
You've made it this far. Now it's just one attempt.
The stadium is bigger, but the lane is the same.
Your body feels like wet concrete. You compete anyway.
Competing on fumes today. You've done it before.
Your legs feel dull in warmups, and you cannot fake freshness.
Low energy turns simple reps into real decisions.
You're a little flat today. It's on you to find it anyway.
Not hurt, just drained. The warmup feels longer.
You can feel the training load in your body.
You are near the edge where effort costs more than usual.
You pushed hard this week. Today's payment shows up early.
Your body isn't complaining, it's just tired.
You know you overdid one session. Now you compete with it.
Everything hurts. You know what you're doing to your body. You do it anyway.
The body's already said no. Your head overrules it.
Everything feels heavy today, including the first steps.
You scale the plan down because survival matters too.
You can feel every step. There's no hiding it today.
This is what overtraining feels like. You line up anyway.
Your body is heavy from the first rep.
You wish you'd backed off earlier in the week.
You know this one is going to hurt. It's already hurting.
The tank is empty. You try to make it count.
You feel sharp today. Warm from the first step.
Everything is clicking. These are the days you remember.
The body feels responsive, and the pace comes easier.
You can push session quality without forcing it.
Fully rested. Your stride is right. Trust the training.
One of those rare days where everything feels easy before it has to be hard.
You feel springy in warmups and sharp in the first rep.
Fresh legs let you focus on execution instead of damage control.
Bye week. You train while others compete.
No meet this week. The body gets what it needs.
They compete. You prepare. Sometimes that's the harder choice.
No race this week, so the work gets quieter and more deliberate.
You treat the bye like a reset instead of a pause.
A week without spikes can still move the season forward.
No meet. You lift, you stretch, you reset.
It's a quiet Saturday. You still go to the track.
You watch results online and take notes.
Without a meet looming, the details finally get full attention.
You leave the track with less noise in your head.
The bye week gives your body time to catch your ambition.
First place. You knew it before the results were posted.
You hold up the win. It's the first of something.
State champion. The title settles over you slowly.
You didn't just win the state meet. You owned it.
National champion. The phrase doesn't feel real yet.
You came to nationals. You won. Everything you sacrificed was worth this.
Nationals podium. You belong in this conversation now.
Top three at nationals. Some people never get here. You did.
State qualified. You check the list twice.
You got the standard. That's a different level now.
State meet on the calendar. It feels real.
Regional qualifier. You earned the next line.
You're moving on. Not everyone does.
Regionals. That's a bigger stage and you made it.
Three wins on your rival this season. That's a statement.
You've taken that matchup over this year.
People notice when you keep beating the same person.
Three wins on {rival_name} this season. That's ownership.
You managed your energy all season. That's discipline.
No very-low weeks. You kept the line.
You stayed healthy enough to keep going every week.
Senior year PB. You still had another gear.
You hit your fastest mark when it mattered most.
Year four and you still found a new best.
Under 12. You see the clock and feel something shift.
Sub-12. You've crossed into different territory now.
Clean over every barrier. That's what it looks like when technique holds.
No errors today. You ran it exactly the way it was drawn up.
Sixty meters. That's real pop in the runway and a clean release.
You watch it carry. You knew it was big the moment it left your hand.
You feel your recovery sharpen. The next week comes faster.
Coach adjusts the plan. You bounce back quicker now.
Your legs come back faster between sessions now.
Coach fine-tunes your rest days. You absorb the work better.
The technique work is paying off. The reps feel clean.
Everything looks a little sharper at full speed.
Pressure sits lighter on your shoulders.
You hold your focus longer than you used to.
You feel the power in the first steps.
Stronger now. You can feel it in the ground.
You are a little quicker from the gun.
Speed is easier to access now.
Coach walks the lanes before the first practice, saying little. The season already has a shape; you just can't see all of it yet.
Week one of the plan is on the whiteboard: base first, speed later. You read it twice and get to work.
Coach keeps the warmup short and the words shorter. Club champs isn't the summit. It's the gate.
You know the assignment without being told: take care of today, and the season keeps going.
The regular season is finished. Every meet was a data point -- and the data says you're getting better.
Eight meets, eight lessons. You showed up every week and that counts for more than it seems.
You earned your spot on the line every single week. That's the foundation everything else is built on.
Club Championship reached. The field thinned and you were still in it.
You made it past the regular season -- that's further than most. Next year you'll know what to expect.
A Class Meet appearance. You're competing at a level that tests what you're made of.
Class level competition. You found out how good you really are -- now you know what to fix.
State. That's a resume line you'll always have.
You competed at the State level. Not many athletes ever get to say that.
The State Meet stage is different. You've been there now -- use that.
Regionals. You outran nearly everyone in the state to be there.
A Regional appearance is elite company. You belong in that conversation.
You made Regionals. This season was something to build on.
You ran at Nationals. That's the pinnacle of high school track -- and you were there.
National level. Whatever happens next, nobody can take this season away from you.
The national stage. You've reached as far as this sport goes at this level. Remember how this feels.
Your rival is already warming up when you arrive. You note it and say nothing.
You see them on the other side of the infield. Same routine, same focus. Good.
Loss to your rival. You don't say much on the bus home.
They got you clean today. No excuses. Just a number to chase.
You finish behind them and feel it in your chest for hours.
You beat your rival today and it wasn't close. That's new.
You cross ahead and don't look back. You already know.
Clean win on your rival. The gap is yours to defend now.
You watch their event before yours. You file it away.
First time you've matched up. Their form is good. So is yours.
The warmup lane feels smaller when they're in it.
You catch their splits from the last meet scrolling through your head.
Same event, same rival. The field notices.
You nod at {rival_name} during warmups. They nod back. That's enough.
{rival_name} is here. You both know what that means.
Respect is easy when it's earned. {rival_name} has earned it.
You've seen their warmup so many times you could run it yourself.
By now, you don't even check the heat sheet. You just know.
Same face, different week. The routine sharpens you.
Four years of this. You'll miss it when it's gone.
You've been chasing each other since freshman year. The finish line keeps moving.
Senior season. {rival_name} across the infield. One last run at it.
{rival_name} is in the field. Your warmup gets two minutes longer.
You see {rival_name} checking the conditions. Competitor's habit.
You breathe out for the first time since warmups.
Win against {rival_name}. The bus ride home is quieter in a good way.
That one mattered and everyone in the field knew it.
You made your point today. {rival_name} heard it.
That wasn't close. {rival_name} knows the gap.
Statement win. You don't need to say anything.
You've been waiting for that matchup all week.
Results posted. Your name above {rival_name}. That's the order now.
Some wins are just wins. That one was proof.
Coach gives you a look after the race. That's the nod that means something.
Teammates saw who you beat. They don't say it out loud. They don't have to.
{rival_name} shakes your hand. Firm grip. They'll be ready next time.
Another one on {rival_name}. The record between you is getting clear.
You're stacking wins in this matchup now.
The head-to-head isn't close anymore. You've made sure of that.
Close enough to taste it. Not close enough to take it.
You replay the last 50 meters on the drive home.
The margin was nothing. The result was everything.
{rival_name} was better today. Simple as that.
You tip your head. They earned that one clean.
No excuses. {rival_name} ran their race and you couldn't answer.
You slam your bag down after the race. Nobody asks.
Second to {rival_name} again. The pattern needs to break.
That one sits in your chest through the whole cool-down.
You write down {rival_name}'s mark before you leave.
Loss logged. Training plan adjusted.
You already know what you need to work on Monday.
{rival_name} is pulling away in this rivalry. You feel the shift.
The gap was bigger today. That's harder to ignore than a close loss.
You're chasing now. That hasn't been true before.
Something about {rival_name}'s warmup tells you this won't be the last time.
You check the results. {rival_name} is right there. Noted.
New name in your head after today: {rival_name}.
{rival_name} looked over at you before the start. You looked back.
First real matchup with {rival_name}. You both came to compete.
Three straight on your rival. The matchup isn't a question anymore.
You own this rivalry right now. They know it.
Win streak on {rival_name}. The field watches different when it's not close.
{rival_name} hasn't beaten you in a while. You can see it in their warmup.
The streak is real. You've figured {rival_name} out.
Second week. The newness is gone. Now it's just work.
Week two. You know the routine. You want better than last week.
The early-season jitters are fading. Good.
Week three. You're starting to find your shape for the season.
Three weeks in. The times are honest now.
You're not surprised by your body anymore. That's progress.
Week six. The season is in the body now. You feel it.
Two more regular meets. You want to be peaking, not just surviving.
This is the stretch where seasons are made or lost.
Second-to-last regular meet. You want sharp, not flashy.
Week seven. One more before the finale. Stay focused.
So close to postseason. Don't leave anything on the track today.
The regular season teaches you something about yourself every week. Today is no different.
Not every meet is a statement. Some are just reps.
Today isn't the story. It's a sentence in it.
You won't remember this meet by name. You'll remember how it felt to show up anyway.
This meet won't define your season. Skipping the effort might.
Some days you race for a place. Today you just race for the rep.
Nobody's grading this one on a curve. That's kind of the point.
League meet. You don't take them lightly. You've seen people slip here.
The scoreboard matters less than the execution today.
You run your race. Not someone else's.
You've seen good athletes slip at meets like this. Don't be the lesson.
Watch the matchups less. Watch your own execution more.
It isn't about who's in the next lane today. It's about your race plan.
Today's job is simple: execute, then do it again next week.
Class meet. You find your name on the heat sheet. You belong here.
The travel is longer. The warmup is quieter. The meet matters more.
First postseason meet. You remember why you put in all those early mornings.
State meet. You've run this race in your head a hundred times.
The facility is bigger. You pretend it isn't.
You look at the field. They're good. So are you.
Regionals. You don't take the bib for granted. You know what it took to get here.
The warmup area is quieter than you expected. Everyone here means business.
You've been to smaller meets with bigger attitudes. This field is different.
